speaker: Host
text: It is May 8th, 2026, and as we all continue to watch the clashes between the U.S. and Iran play out in the Strait of Hormuz, the question remains, do we actually have a ceasefire here?

speaker: Host
text: Because while you do see this back and forth, it seems as though the U.S. and Iran are up until now hesitant to really go up the escalation ladder. But just how long is that going to last, especially as you have Trump?

speaker: Host
text: Set to visit Beijing next week for direct talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Because it's no secret that part of this war on Iran, part of this U.S. blockade of the Iranian blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is about trying to hurt China,

speaker: Host
text: trying to cut off the resources, particularly the oil trade that is headed directly to China that Beijing relies on. So what does all of this mean for where the conflicts?

speaker: Host
text: Conflict stands and what should we be keeping an eye on? Well, we got into all of that and more with a special guest earlier. So let's take a listen to that conversation. Now joining me now to discuss is Professor John Mearsheimer, an author, international relations expert, and professor at the University of Chicago. Professor Mearsheimer, thanks so much for taking the time to join me.

speaker: Guest
text: You're welcome, Rachel.

speaker: Host
text: Great to have you back on the show. Now, I want to get your take on the latest here. I know that the news has been all over the place over the last 24 hours, but we have Iran announcing that it launched strikes against three U.S. Navy destroyers. The U.S. reported to have launched strikes against empty Iranian tankers with Iranian media saying that those were actually civilian boats.

speaker: Host
text: Yet the Trump administration is acting as if this ceasefire is. is still in place that a deal is on its way to end the war we're all kind of waiting and watching to see what Iran is going to do about this reported U.S. proposal so to start out how do you view the current state of this conflict and how close could we be to this ceasefire officially being declared over?

speaker: Guest
text: Let's go back to last Sunday when President Trump announced Project Freedom. Up until then, it looked like. Like we did basically have a ceasefire between the United States on one side and the Iranians on the other side.

speaker: Guest
text: We certainly still had these two blockades on, the American blockade on the Strait of Hormuz and the Iranian blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. But there was no shooting for the time being. And then on Sunday, President Trump said. That we were going to guide those tankers and other cargo ships that were in the Persian Gulf out of the Gulf so that they could deliver their goods around the world.

speaker: Guest
text: And he didn't say that we were going to use military force or we're going to open the Strait with military force, but we were going to quote unquote guide those ships out. Then on Monday, we attempted to do that, and we ended up in a shooting match.

speaker: Guest
text: There were Iranian boats and American destroyers and American aircraft that engaged in firefights in the Strait of Hormuz. And very importantly, the Iranians also attacked the United Arab Emirates. They attacked this port, a very important point.

speaker: Guest
text: Port of Fujaira, where the UAE has been exporting its oil from because that port, Fujaira, is outside of the Persian Gulf, but the Iranians attacked it. And this is what happened on Monday. Then on Tuesday, President Trump said epic fury is taking a pause. It's over with. So we had no more shooting on Tuesday or on Wednesday. And then yesterday, which was Thursday, it started up again.

speaker: Guest
text: And it appears that three American destroyers tried to go through the Strait. I believe they were leaving the Persian Gulf and going into the Gulf of Oman. And the Iranians fired on them. And the United States retaliated by hitting three targets inside of Iran. It was not a major escalation by any means. many means, but nevertheless, it was an escalation for sure. So that was yesterday.

speaker: Guest
text: And basically what we're seeing here is that Thursday looked like Monday. And then today, Friday, we're back at it again. It appears that the United States has hit two empty Iranian tankers that were trying to go through the blockade. Furthermore, it appears that the Iranians are again striking the United Arab Emirates. So the fighting has started again today, Friday, but at a very low level.

speaker: Guest
text: So I think what you see, Rachel, over the course of this week, going from Monday to today, Friday, is that the ceasefire really doesn't exist in the literal sense of that term. I mean, if you cease fire, then you cease firing at each other.

speaker: Guest
text: And this is not a ceasefire. But at the same time, it's very low level. These are very low level engagements. And it's not a case of the United States or Iran marching up the escalation ladder in a really meaningful way. And I think that what most people fear is that that's what's going to eventually happen. But it certainly has not happened yet.

speaker: Host
text: Yeah, and it has been so interesting to watch exactly as you're describing it there is it's like every day we're getting up going, okay, what has happened now?

speaker: Host
text: And it almost feels like we're on the edge of a cliff and we're just waiting for one of the sides to launch a massive attack and then the other side to retaliate in that escalation ladder to speed up very, very quickly compared to what we've seen. And it has been interesting to watch as the Trump administration seems to really have this focus on this naval blockade of Iranian ports as if that in and of itself is not an act of war,

speaker: Host
text: as if it's not. If it's much more of a humanitarian mission here, and I thought it was interesting, you have the Washington Post coming out and reporting this week that a confidential CIA analysis was delivered to administration policymakers this week, which concludes that Iran can survive the U.S. naval blockade for at least three to four months before facing more severe economic hardship.

speaker: Host
text: So when it. We look at this current situation. I know we've talked in the past about how the global economy is already starting to scream. The idea of this going on for four more months puts us in an even more dire situation. How do you view this current standoff and what it could mean for the economy if the U.S. continues to focus on this blockade?

speaker: Guest
text: A couple points. One is just to go to the Washington Post story about the CIA report. a report. Basically, that report says that the Iranian economy will be wrecked in three to four months, or it'll take three to four months to really wreck the Iranian economy. But that very importantly does not mean that Iran will surrender.

speaker: Guest
text: And I believe that Iran will not surrender, that you can inflict much more punishment beyond those three or four months. And the Iranians will not surrender. I think the historical record is quite clear on this issue,

speaker: Guest
text: that societies in wartime can absorb enormous amounts of punishment, number one. And number two, the Iranians are facing an existential threat. They're up against two genocidal states, the United States and Israel,

speaker: Guest
text: and they are committed to destroying Iran, to wrecking it, to making Iran look like Syria. And given... Iran's emphasis on survival, they're not going to surrender. So this is going to go on beyond three to four months. Second point I would make is that the proponents of the blockade are fully aware that it will take a long time to work.

speaker: Guest
text: From their perspective, in other words, they don't think that Iran is going to throw up its hands tomorrow. They understand that it's going to take, even in their story, a long time. And that has led them to put an emphasis on restarting the bombing campaign against Iran. And in other words, the belief is that the blockade in conjunction with a bombing campaign a serious.

speaker: Guest
text: bombing campaign will be a formidable one-two punch that will finish off the Iranians and get them to surrender. So I think the reason that you and I both wake up every day wondering whether or not we're going to go up the escalation ladder today or not is because there is a lot of pressure on the administration from the proponents of this war to go up the escalation ladder and to start

speaker: Guest
text: start bombing Iran again because they all understand deep down that the blockade alone is not going to do the trick certainly in a short period of time and as you said the more time goes on the more the economy

speaker: Guest
text: begins to move in and crush President Trump and put him in a position where he has no choice but to figure out how to get out of this conflict. So the crushing effects of the continuing war do create an incentive for him to try to escalate.

speaker: Guest
text: But the problem that he faces is that escalation is not a war-winning strategy. The fact is that we bombed once before and it didn't. And it didn't work. Now, the other side will argue,

speaker: Guest
text: yes, but the last time we bombed, we did not have simultaneously a blockade on Iran. Now we have the one-two punch. So let's give it a try. And that logic may be enough to push Trump over the line and get him to go up the escalation ladder.

speaker: Guest
text: But as I have long argued, that's not going to work. The fact is, as I said before, you can inflict. flicked massive punishment on the Iranians and given their strategic situation, they're not going to quit.

speaker: Host
text: Yeah, exactly. As you said, as I'm watching the Trump administration, it's almost more concerning to me, the people that are in his ear that are telling him, oh, yeah, yeah, we can do it now. We've got this blockcade. We've got all these different military options. But exactly as you're describing it there,

speaker: Host
text: look, Iran is holding on to control of the Strait of Hormuz with everything they have. That is not going to change. I mean, the IRGC announced this week that they will be expanding their control of the Strait, going right up to the UAE's port,

speaker: Host
text: which you were explaining earlier they're using to bypass this initial blockade by Iran. So when it comes to control of the Strait of Hormuz and the impact that Iran is able to have on the global economy, on all of these different elements. that we all rely on. We obviously have not started feeling anywhere near the pain we are going to feel just yet.

speaker: Host
text: How important is this vital waterway and do you expect to see the conflict continue to focus on the Strait of Hormuz as the U.S. and Iran continue to face off?

speaker: Guest
text: You want to remember that to get a deal here so that we can end up putting this. conflict behind us and avoiding the international economy going off a cliff, there are just all sorts of issues that have to be worked out.

speaker: Guest
text: I mean, one of the reasons I'm very pessimistic that you're going to get some sort of meaningful settlement that will allow the international economy not to go off a cliff is because there are just so many different issues.

speaker: Guest
text: that have to be settled before you can end the conflict. Now, a lot of focus on the part of the Trump administration is on the nuclear issue. And the nuclear issue by itself is a very complicated issue.

speaker: Guest
text: There's the whole question of what kind of nuclear enrichment capability the Iranians are going to be allowed to have. There's the whole question of what to do with... Those 11 tons of enriched uranium that the Iranians have.

speaker: Guest
text: There's the question that you were raising of the Strait of Hormuz. What's the future of the Strait of Hormuz? Is the United States going to allow Iran to control the Strait of Hormuz and keep a toll booth in that strait for the foreseeable future? What's the future of American military bases in the region? What's the future of reparations?

speaker: Guest
text: what's the future of sanctions what's the future of Iran's relationship with Hezbollah Hamas and the Houthis these are all big issues what's the future of the Iranian missile program and so forth and so on so we not only have to work out the nuclear issue and the question of who controls the Strait all these issues have to be addressed And it's hard for me to imagine the United States and Iran.

speaker: Guest
text: reaching a deal on these different issues anytime soon and then throw in the Israelis even if the United States and the Iranians are moving toward a deal the Israelis who want to continue the war and want to destroy Iran very much will go to great lengths to undermine the negotiations between the United States and Iran and then when it comes to negotiations there are two big problems there

speaker: Host
text: One is the trust issue. Do you think the Iranians trust the United States at this point in time? The last time the two sides were negotiating, we all of a sudden launched a war against Iran in the middle of the negotiation.

speaker: Host
text: Can the Iranians trust the United States? And then moreover. Do you think the Trump administration is really capable of executing a negotiation that leads to a meaningful settlement? This is the gang that can't shoot straight.

speaker: Guest
text: They couldn't settle the war in Ukraine, right? They can't end the genocide in Gaza. I mean, this is not a highly competent administration that's filled with top rate. diplomats. Instead,

speaker: Guest
text: they have people like Jared Kushner and Steve Whitcoff in charge. So when you put all these things together, it's just hard to see how you get any meaningful settlement. And if you don't get a meaningful settlement, the economic consequences of this in the months ahead are just going to increase greatly. Almost everybody agrees that

speaker: Guest
text: The longer this war goes on, the worse the economic consequences for countries all around the world. And President Trump seems to understand that. He seems very anxious to figure out some way to get out of this pickle that he's in. But as I said, I don't see how he does that. There's just so many factors that weigh against a meaningful agreement. I hope I'm wrong, but I don't think I am.

speaker: Host
text: Oh, I'm with you on that one. And that when you look at this and yeah, in addition to the fact that the U.S. only seems to have two diplomats that can do anything here when it comes to the Trump administration,

speaker: Host
text: you also have the fact that they were on the brink of a deal multiple times with Iran and each time they decided to attack in the middle of negotiations. Now, while all of that is happening, I do also want to bring up Trump's planned visit to Beijing next week because he's set to have talks with.

speaker: Host
text: Chinese President Xi Jinping. The stakes really couldn't be higher there as you look at the role that China is kind of playing in all of this and where it stands. And we also had Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Arachi in China for talks this week. And there has been, of course, increasing talk about this turning into an energy war, especially with a focus on the Strait of Hormuz.

speaker: Host
text: I mean, as of last year, it was estimated that around half of the world's energy was coming from China. Around half of China's crude oil imports were coming from the Middle East. So how do you see the position that China is playing here, its ability to talk to the U.S., but also this meeting coming at a time when the U.S. is in a position of openly trying to hurt China here?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, I think that President Trump is clearly in a weak position here. I think one of the reasons that he'd like to settle this one quickly.

speaker: Guest
text: One of the reasons he wants to get the Iranians to buy on to his plan and start seriously negotiating over his plan is so that he can go to Beijing and look like he's in the process of putting an end to this war and he can look like a peacemaker and he can present a positive image to Xi Jinping. But the fact is, that's unlikely to happen.

speaker: Guest
text: The idea that he's going to be in a strong position because the Iranians are caving into him and willing to agree to some sort of settlement on Trump's terms is not going to happen.

speaker: Guest
text: So he's going to go to Beijing in a weak position because the war is going to be continuing and Xi Jinping is going to be in a position. position where he can remind President Trump that he is responsible for starting this war,

speaker: Guest
text: that it was a fundamental mistake, and that the entire world is suffering, not just China and not just the United States. And therefore, it's imperative that he, President Trump,

speaker: Guest
text: end the war as quickly as possible. So what I think Xi Jinping will do is try to make it clear that Trump's responsible for the war. And he bears the burden of ending the war. Xi Jinping will say, I've been very helpful up to now.

speaker: Guest
text: I've put pressure on both the Pakistanis and the Iranians to meet in Islamabad last month. But there are limits to what I can do. You're in the driver's seat, President Trump, and you have to end this war.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, President Trump doesn't know how he can end the war. That's the problem he faces. So you can see that. that President Trump will be in a very awkward position when he goes to Beijing. Make one final point.

speaker: Guest
text: The United States is deeply involved, obviously, in this war in the Middle East. It's also deep. deeply involved in the war in Ukraine. It's trying to wean itself off that war and shift the burden to the Europeans. But nevertheless,

speaker: Guest
text: the United States remains a key player in that war. That means, given that we're involved in Europe and in the Persian Gulf, that the last thing President Trump wants is a crisis in East Asia. That would be a bridge too far, to put it mildly. So I think that means that President Trump will go to great lengths to be nice.

speaker: Guest
text: to Xi Jinping and to smooth over ruffled feathers and to make it clear that he wants, he President Trump wants to have good relations with China. Because again, the last thing that President Trump needs is a crisis or a conflict, a shooting conflict in East Asia. So again, you see that Xi Jinping is in the driver's seat here.

speaker: Host
text: Yeah, and I think that that's an incredibly important point as we watch this meeting come together. And I wanted to get into kind of the position of U.S. foreign policy strategy here, because exactly as you're describing it there,

speaker: Host
text: the U.S. is trying to put this focus over here on West Asia, on the war in Iran, and in doing so they're having to take away from their focus on Europe, from their focus on East Asia.

speaker: Host
text: And it reminds me of the fact that the U.S. gleefully encouraged its allies in Europe. up to cut off their reliance on cheap natural resources from Russia and to instead increase their reliance on the U.S. and also the Middle East,

speaker: Host
text: now the U.S. is pursuing this war against Iran that not only hurts Europe, but it hurts U.S. allies in the Persian Gulf and U.S. allies in East Asia. And so when we look at the future of the U.S. and its ability to project power,

speaker: Host
text: it feels as though right now the U.S. is making all of its all of its top allies even weaker while at the same time also making itself weaker. How do you view this overall strategy and kind of what the U.S. is capable of here?

speaker: Guest
text: I'm going to just basically repeat what you said because I agree completely and I'll just try to put a finer point on it or reinforce what you said. But, you know, as you described it, there are three sets of allies here that are at play, our allies in East Asia. our allies in Europe and our allies in the Gulf. And as a result of what happened on February 28th when we went to war against Iran,

speaker: Guest
text: we have badly hurt all of our allies just to go to East Asia because we are running out of weapons and ammunition in the Persian Gulf. We are actually moving patriots.

speaker: Guest
text: And add missile batteries out of East Asia to the Gulf, we are moving or we have moved a marine expeditionary unit out of Japan to the Gulf. We're in effect pivoting away from East Asia and putting our allies in a precarious position.

speaker: Guest
text: So we're doing real damage there. And furthermore, and this cannot be underestimated. The economic effects of this war have been felt much more in East Asia than either Europe or in the United States.

speaker: Guest
text: So if you look at Japan and South Korea, they're really hurting as a result of the economic consequences of the war. So just to sum up the situation in East Asia, we're damaging our allies both from a strategic perspective.

speaker: Guest
text: From a strategic point of view, because we're pivoting away from East Asia and from an economic point of view because of all the pain that we're causing them on the economic front, then you go to Europe, you describe the situation well there,

speaker: Guest
text: the Ukraine war. was a disaster for the European economies. And this war now is doing significant damage, especially in Britain, and will do greater damage with the passage of time. Then you go to the Gulf. On February 27th, we had this very...

speaker: Guest
text: What looked like impressive alliance structure involving the GCC states, the Gulf Council states, Cooperation Council states, and we had bases in almost all those states.

speaker: Guest
text: And it looked like we really had a formidable deterrent structure in place since the war began. Almost all those bases have either been badly damaged or destroyed. These are the American bases. And furthermore, instead of providing security for. Our allies in the Gulf, our bases have acted like a giant magnet that attracts Iranian ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, and drones.

speaker: Guest
text: Furthermore, you see all sorts of fractures taking place among the GCC states. And the most obvious example is what's happened with the UAE and Saudi Arabia. It seems that the UAE views Saudi Arabia as very much. every much as big a threat as Iran.

speaker: Guest
text: And the Saudis probably view the UAE as an even bigger threat than Iran. And that's just the beginning. So our alliance structure, our basing structure in the Gulf has been badly damaged,

speaker: Guest
text: to put it mildly. So to add all these things up, if you look at what happened to our alliance structure in East Asia, in Europe, and in the Persian Gulf. We've done serious damage to all three of these alliance structures. At the same time, we are pursuing a losing war.

speaker: Host
text: Yeah, that's the scary part there, right? Especially when you look at the position that the U.S. is in, all of the damage it has caused. And that brings me to my next point, which is kind of what comes next when you look at the overall multipolar world. You've got China and Russia in this position where... where they're being very careful right now, right?

speaker: Host
text: The fact that you have Trump's trip to China still happening, you've got Beijing in this position where they're kind of carefully handling someone who we know is a bit volatile, and that raises concerns about what the U.S. could do,

speaker: Host
text: what Israel could do if this escalates to the point where they realize that not only are they not in the driver's seat, but there's no way for them to get back to it, and you have this growing multipolar world that is continuing to put them in their place.

speaker: Host
text: So the last thing I wanted to bring up, as you watch this trip kind of come together in the next week, and as we watch the possibility of the U.S., whether it's returning to strikes or an attempted special ops ground invasion of sorts in Iran, what are you going to be keeping an eye on when it comes to that kind of overall strategy of the multipolar world in dealing? dealing with the U.S.

speaker: Guest
text: Yeah, that's a great question. If you listen to me talk up to now, especially when you and I were both describing the situation with our allies in the different regions of the world,

speaker: Guest
text: one might get the impression that these are three distinct regions and there are few interconnections among them. But if you listen carefully to what I was saying and you really think about it. These regions are interconnected in all sorts of ways.

speaker: Guest
text: And when you talk about the war in the Gulf, for example, it's very important to understand that the Russians and the Chinese are both helping Iran wage the war. I believe one of the reasons that the missile campaign, excuse me, that Iran has launched against the Gulf states and against Israel and against America. American military bases.

speaker: Guest
text: One of the reasons it has been so effective is because of the intelligence that the Russians have been providing the Iranians. And of course, the Chinese have been providing weaponry and military help as well. Furthermore, as you well know,

speaker: Guest
text: And as you pointed out, China is heavily dependent on imports, imported oil and other kinds of imports as well from the Persian Gulf. So you see that China and Russia are inextricably bound up in the events that are taking place in the Gulf.

speaker: Guest
text: And, of course, the Russians and the Chinese work together all the time, especially in. in Ukraine, where the Chinese have helped the Russians in a variety of ways. So all of these conflict situations are interconnected. And the great danger is that through some happenstance, we'll end up in a war.

speaker: Guest
text: In the Persian Gulf that involves Russia and China or Russia or China, in addition to the Persian Gulf states, in addition to Iran. So my point is we have to be remarkably. careful here to make sure that the conflict in the Persian Gulf doesn't escalate to the point where both the Chinese and the Russians come in. And I think that's a real danger.

speaker: Guest
text: And given the fact that it doesn't appear that you're going to be able to settle this conflict permanently. and that it's going to linger for a long, long time and that China and Russia are not going to lose their interest in the Persian Gulf. The danger of escalation to the point where all the great powers or two of the great powers get involved is very real.

speaker: Guest
text: So the point I'm making here in general is we want to be aware that there are three different areas of the world and you can look at them in isolation, but ultimately they're interrelated or interconnected in very important ways.

speaker: Host
text: Oh, absolutely. And exactly as you're describing it there, it is raising a lot of concerns about all of the different ways that this conflict could escalate. And the U.S. is involved in every single element here,

speaker: Host
text: picking all of these fights and now starting to see a lot of the consequences of its foreign policy actions here. I know that there is certainly a lot at stake here all around. And I really appreciate you taking the time to join me.

speaker: Host
text: me today to break down all of the latest here. John Mearsheimer, an author, international relations expert, and professor at the University of Chicago. Thank you so much for your time and insight.

speaker: Guest
text: My pleasure, Rachel. Thank you for having me on the show.

speaker: Host
